step_by_step_thinking:
\boxed{B}
**Clinical Reasoning:** According to the evidence, Class 2 hemorrhagic shock corresponds to blood loss of 15%–30% of total blood volume, where hypotension typically begins to develop alongside elevated heart and respiratory rates. While earlier classes show minimal or no hypotension, the onset of measurable hypotension aligns with this range. Although later stages involve more profound hypotension, the earliest clinically evident hypotension following significant blood loss falls within this threshold. Therefore, hypotension generally occurs when blood loss exceeds 15%, marking the transition from compensated to decompensated shock.
